root_text.sn45.2:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sakyesu viharati nagarakaṁ nāma sakyānaṁ nigamo.
root_text.sn45.2:1.3: Atha kho āyasmā ānando yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā ānando bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn45.2:2.2: Sakalamevidaṁ, ānanda, brahmacariyaṁ, yadidaṁ—kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatā.
root_text.sn45.2:2.3: Kalyāṇamittassetaṁ, ānanda, bhikkhuno pāṭikaṅkhaṁ kalyāṇasahāyassa kalyāṇasampavaṅkassa—ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāvessati, ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkarissati.
root_text.sn45.2:3.1: Kathañcānanda, bhikkhu kalyāṇamitto kalyāṇasahāyo kalyāṇasampavaṅko ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti, ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaroti?
root_text.sn45.2:3.2: Idhānanda, bhikkhu sammādiṭṭhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ;
root_text.sn45.2:3.9: sammāsamādhiṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.
root_text.sn45.2:3.10: Evaṁ kho, ānanda, bhikkhu kalyāṇamitto kalyāṇasahāyo kalyāṇasampavaṅko ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bhāveti, ariyaṁ aṭṭhaṅgikaṁ maggaṁ bahulīkaroti.
root_text.sn45.2:4.1: Tadamināpetaṁ, ānanda, pariyāyena veditabbaṁ yathā sakalamevidaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, yadidaṁ—kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatā.
root_text.sn45.2:4.2: Mamañhi, ānanda, kalyāṇamittaṁ āgamma jātidhammā sattā jātiyā parimuccanti; jarādhammā sattā jarāya parimuccanti; maraṇadhammā sattā maraṇena parimuccanti; sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsadhammā sattā sokaparidevadukkhadomanassupāyāsehi parimuccanti.
root_text.sn45.2:4.3: Iminā kho etaṁ, ānanda, pariyāyena veditabbaṁ yathā sakalamevidaṁ brahmacariyaṁ, yadidaṁ—kalyāṇamittatā kalyāṇasahāyatā kalyāṇasampavaṅkatā”ti.

translation_text.sn45.2:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying in the land of the Sakyans, where they have a town named Townsville.
translation_text.sn45.2:1.3: Then Venerable Ānanda went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn45.2:1.4: “Sir, good friends, companions, and associates are half the spiritual life.”
translation_text.sn45.2:2.2: Good friends, companions, and associates are the whole of the spiritual life.
translation_text.sn45.2:2.3: A mendicant with good friends, companions, and associates can expect to develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path.
translation_text.sn45.2:3.1: And how does a mendicant with good friends develop and cultivate the noble eightfold path?
translation_text.sn45.2:3.2: It’s when a mendicant develops right view, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go.
translation_text.sn45.2:3.9: right immersion, which relies on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripens as letting go.
translation_text.sn45.2:3.10: That’s how a mendicant with good friends develops and cultivates the noble eightfold path.
translation_text.sn45.2:4.1: And here’s another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life.
translation_text.sn45.2:4.2: For, by relying on me as a good friend, sentient beings who are liable to rebirth, old age, and death, to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress are freed from all these things.
translation_text.sn45.2:4.3: This is another way to understand how good friends are the whole of the spiritual life.”

variant_text.sn45.2:1.2: sakyesu → sakkesu (bj) | nagarakaṁ nāma → nāgarakaṁ nāma (bj); sakkaraṁ nāma (sya-all, pts1ed, mr)

reference_text.sn45.2:4.1: bj17.1.6, csp1ed14.3, csp2ed14.3, ms14S5_9, ndp15.5, pts-vp-pli5.3
